FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD), N=7514, ages 9–10 years at baseline 2016–2018, 21 U.S. research sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective modified Poisson regression models estimating associations between social media time (Year 1) and alcohol sipping (Year 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Substance Use & Misuse, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (self-reported hours per day) is IV in primary model; alcohol sipping (Timeline Follow-Back) is DV. Individual-trait-level outcome: substance use behavior (alcohol initiation). Qualifies as smartphone/SM use paired with within-person behavioral construct.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression Model 1: social media time RR 1.31 (95% CI 1.20–1.43), p<0.001 for alcohol sipping

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: direct effects (RR 1.31, p<0.001) and indirect effect through problematic SM use (B=0.011, BC 95% CI 0.007–0.016, p<0.001), mediation proportion 25.0%

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design confirmed (Years 1–3). Social media is IV in Model 1 and Model 3; therefore SM appears as IV in at least one analysis. EC7 does not apply when SM is IV anywhere.
notes: Strong prospective evidence. Mediation analysis enriches understanding of mechanism (problematic SM use partially mediates SM time–alcohol sipping association).

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=4,718 U.S. adolescents aged 10–15 years, Years 2–3 (2018–2021)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariate linear regression models estimating joint associations of screen time and physical activity (steps) with CVD risk factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in BMC Public Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is defined as composite 'recreational screen time hours per day' bundling television shows, YouTube videos, videogames, texting, video chatting, AND social media with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM activity. Methods state: 'Types of media included television shows or movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, social media, and texting.' No method reported for isolating phone/SM time from this multi-device, multi-platform composite. Violates FT-IC2 §2.4: 'Composite "screen time" totals that bundle TV/console/phone with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM' do NOT qualify.

notes: Paper measures composite recreational screen time (TV + videos + games + texting + video chat + social media) without isolating phone or SM components. Even though outcomes are cardiovascular health markers (individual-trait-level), the exposure does not meet FT-IC2 requirements.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=9,538 adolescents, 9–10 years at baseline 2016–2018, two-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effects models examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and parent-reported mental health symptoms (CBCL)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in BMC Public Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary independent variable is total screen time, measured as composite aggregate of multiple media types with no per-modality breakdown. Methods state: 'Types of media included television shows or movies, watching/streaming videos [e.g., YouTube], playing videogames, texting, video chatting [e.g., Skype, Facetime], and social media [e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter].' Total screen time bundles TV, videos, videogames, texting, video chat, and SM without isolating phone or SM time. Although secondary analyses examine specific screen types (video chat, texting, videos, video games), the primary model tested in main analyses uses total composite screen time. Violates FT-IC2 §2.4: composite screen time bundling TV/phone with no per-modality breakdown.

notes: Paper uses total aggregate screen time (TV + videos + games + texting + video chat + social media) as primary IV, with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM. Although mental health outcomes are individual-trait-level and specific screen types are examined in secondary analyses, the main exposure variable does not meet FT-IC2 conceptual threshold.
